cues.x: Welcome.
Today we are digging into "100 Million Dollar Money Models" by Alex Hormozi.
If you have been following along, this is the fourth book in his Acquisition dot com series.
It comes after "100 Million Dollar Offers" and "100 Million Dollar Leads," and it might just be the piece that ties the whole engine together.
So let me set the table for you before we get into the meat of it.
Think of it like this.
In "Offers," Hormozi taught you what to sell.
He taught you how to build what he calls a Grand Slam Offer, something so good that people feel stupid saying no.
In "Leads," he taught you how to get people to know that offer exists, how to open a flood of strangers pointing their attention at your business.
But there is a gap that both of those books leave open, and it is a gap that quietly kills more businesses than bad products or weak marketing ever do.
That gap is money.
Not profit on a spreadsheet.
Actual cash, in the bank, in your hands, fast enough to keep the machine running.
And that is exactly what "Money Models" is about.
It teaches you how to structure the money.
The sequence of offers, the timing of payments, the way you turn a total stranger into a paying customer so quickly and so profitably that your growth pays for itself.
This is the book about getting paid faster so you can outspend everyone else in your market and take it over.
So settle in, because this one is dense, and it is worth every minute.
Let me start with the core problem, because if you understand the problem clearly, everything else in the book snaps into focus.
Most businesses do not die because they are unprofitable on paper.
Let me say that again, because it is counterintuitive and it is the heartbeat of this entire book.
Most businesses die with a profit showing on their books.
They die because they run out of cash.
Those are two completely different things, and the difference between them is timing.
Here is how it happens.
You spend money today to acquire a customer.
Maybe you run ads, maybe you pay a salesperson, maybe you give away a discount to get the first sale.
That money leaves your account right now, in the present, in cold hard cash.
But the money that customer pays you often comes back to you slowly.
Maybe they are on a monthly plan and they pay you a little bit each month for a year.
Maybe your profit margin on that first sale is thin and you only really make your money on the second, third, and fourth purchases down the road.
So you have this mismatch.
Cash goes out fast.
Cash comes back slow.
Now here is the trap, and it is a cruel one.
The better your marketing works, the worse this problem gets.
Think about that.

If you have a business where you spend a hundred dollars to get a customer and it takes you six months to earn that hundred dollars back, then every single customer you acquire puts you deeper into a hole for those six months.
So what happens when you get really good at acquiring customers?
What happens when the marketing from the "Leads" book starts working and you can suddenly get ten times as many customers?
You go ten times deeper into the hole.
Growth, the very thing you have been chasing, starts to strangle you.
The faster you grow, the more cash you need up front to fund all those customers you are acquiring but have not yet been fully paid by.
This is why you see companies that are growing like wildfire suddenly collapse.
They were not losing money in the accounting sense.
They just ran out of cash before the slow trickle of customer payments could catch up.
Growth ate them alive.
So the whole game, the entire point of a money model, is to fix that timing.
To close the gap between when cash goes out and when cash comes back in.
And if you can close that gap completely, if you can get your money back before you have to spend the next batch, then something magical happens.
Growth stops costing you money and starts funding itself.
You are no longer betting your own scarce cash to grow.
The customers themselves are handing you the fuel to go get the next customer.
That, right there, is the promise of this book.
Now let me give you the central metric, the number that Hormozi wants tattooed on the inside of your eyelids.
He wants you to focus on how fast you get your customer acquisition cost back.
Your customer acquisition cost, which people shorten to CAC, is simply all the money you spend to get one new customer.
Ads, sales commissions, the discount on the first order, all of it added up and divided by the number of customers it produced.
For most business owners, the number they obsess over is whether they eventually make more than that CAC.
And sure, that matters.
But Hormozi says you are watching the wrong clock.
The question that actually determines whether you live or die is not just whether you make your CAC back.
It is how fast.
And the specific target he pushes, the one he built his fortune on, is what you might call thirty day cash.
The idea is that within roughly the first thirty days of acquiring a customer, you want to make back more than you spent to get them.
Not eventually.
Not over the lifetime of the relationship.
Within about a month.
Let me put round numbers on it so it lands.
Say it costs you a hundred dollars to acquire a customer.
Under the slow model, that customer pays you twenty dollars a month, so it takes you five months just to break even on the acquisition, and only after that do you start making real profit.
During those five months, every new customer is a cash drain.
But now imagine you restructure things so that in the first thirty days, that same customer pays you a hundred and fifty dollars.
Now, within a month, you have your hundred dollars back plus fifty extra.
What does that mean?
It means that customer just paid for themselves and handed you fifty dollars in profit, and you can immediately take your hundred dollars and go buy another customer.
And another.
And another.
Your growth is no longer limited by how much cash you have.
It is limited only by how many customers are out there to acquire.

This is what Hormozi means when he says great money models make customer acquisition effectively free, and even better than free, because the customer is paying you a premium for the privilege of being acquired.
He calls this idea client financed acquisition.
And if you read his earlier work you have heard that phrase before, but in this book he expands it from a clever concept into a full operating system.
The core idea is beautiful in its simplicity.
Instead of financing your growth out of your own pocket, or out of a bank loan, or out of investor money, you finance it out of the customers themselves.
The revenue from your new customers, arriving fast enough, pays for the acquisition of the next wave of customers.
The clients finance the acquisition.
You are just the one who set up the machine that lets it happen.
Now, to build that machine, you need to understand the levers.
Because a money model is not one offer.
It is a sequence of offers arranged deliberately so that the total amount a customer pays you, and the speed at which they pay it, both climb as high as possible.
Hormozi breaks these offers into a few distinct types, and I want to walk you through each one carefully, because this is the practical heart of the book.
The first type is the attraction offer, sometimes called the front end offer.
This is the offer whose entire job is to get a customer to buy from you for the very first time.
Notice, that is a different job than making a big profit.
The attraction offer exists to convert a stranger into a buyer.
Because there is a massive psychological gulf between someone who has never given you a dollar and someone who has.
Once a person has bought from you once, once they have handed over their credit card and had a good experience, they are dramatically more likely to buy from you again.
So the front end offer is designed to be almost irresistible, to get that first yes.
Now, a lot of business owners get scared here, because they think the front end offer has to be profitable on its own.
It does not.
In fact, some of the smartest front end offers barely break even, or even lose a little on paper, because their real job is to pay for the advertising that brought the customer in.
Hormozi talks about liquidating offers, which is a front end offer priced specifically so that the cash it brings in covers your ad spend.
Think about how powerful that is.

If your front end offer brings in exactly enough money to pay for the ads that generated it, then you are acquiring customers for free at the point of that first transaction, and everything that comes after, every upsell and every rebill, is pure gravy.
You have essentially handed your ad platform a dollar and had it hand you back a customer and a dollar.
He also loves a specific flavor of attraction offer he sometimes frames as win your money back.
The idea is you give the customer a compelling reason to take an action, and if they do, they earn their money back, or a credit, or a bonus.
It gets people over the hump of the first purchase, and it builds engagement, because now they are motivated to actually use what they bought.
The through line for all attraction offers is the same, though.
Lower the resistance to that first yes as far as you possibly can, even at the cost of front end profit, because that first yes is the doorway to everything else.
The second lever, and this is where the money really starts to move, is the upsell.
The upsell is an offer you present right after the customer says yes to the first thing.
And the timing here is not accidental.
It is everything.
Hormozi talks about the psychology of what he calls the point of greatest deprivation, or the moment of maximum buying momentum.
Here is the insight.
The single best time to sell someone something is immediately after they have just bought something.
It feels backwards, right?
You would think a person who just spent money is tapped out.
But it is the opposite.

In the moment right after a purchase, the customer's guard is down, their wallet is already out, they have already decided that yes, this problem is worth solving and yes, you are the one to solve it.
The decision to trust you has already been made.
So the friction on the next offer is almost nothing.
Think about how you feel when you buy something you have wanted.
There is a rush, a momentum, a sense of I am finally handling this.
That is the exact window where the right additional offer feels like a natural extension rather than a pushy add on.
So you present the upsell.
Maybe it is a premium version.
Maybe it is the thing that makes the first thing work even better.
Maybe it is faster results, more support, done for you instead of do it yourself.

Two classic forms here are the order bump, which is a small add on the customer can check a box for right there on the order form, and the immediate upsell, which is a bigger offer presented on the very next screen or in the very next moment after the sale.
Let me put numbers on it again.
Say your front end offer is fifty dollars and it just covers your ad cost, so you are at break even.
Then, right after the sale, you present a two hundred dollar upsell, and let us say thirty percent of buyers take it.
On average, across all your customers, that upsell is adding sixty dollars of revenue on top of the fifty.
Suddenly your average customer is worth a hundred and ten dollars in that first transaction, most of it profit, and remember the ads were already paid for by the front end.
That sixty dollars of upsell money, arriving in the first few minutes, is a huge chunk of your thirty day cash.
This is why the upsell is such a powerful lever.
It stacks revenue at the exact moment of lowest resistance, and it arrives instantly.
The third lever is the downsell.
Now the downsell is for the people who say no.
And here is a mistake almost every business makes.
When a customer says no to the main offer, most businesses just let them walk away.
They treat a no as the end of the conversation.
Hormozi says a no to the main offer is not a no to everything.
It is very often just a no to that price, or that commitment, or that format, right now.
So the downsell is a smaller, cheaper, or more flexible version of the thing, offered to the person who just declined the bigger one.
There are a few shapes a downsell can take.
It might be a stripped down version at a lower price, so someone who could not stomach the full package can still get in the door with a lighter version.

It might be a payment plan, where you take the same total price but break it into smaller chunks so the sticker shock disappears and the person who could not pay a thousand dollars today can pay a few hundred a month.
It might be a smaller first step that leads to the bigger thing later.
The point is, you are capturing revenue from people who otherwise would have given you nothing.
And that revenue is close to free, because you already paid to get their attention.
They were already standing in front of you.
Letting them leave empty handed is just throwing away money you already spent to earn their consideration.
Picture it.
Out of a hundred people who see your main offer, maybe twenty say yes.
The old way, the other eighty are gone.
The money model way, you offer those eighty a downsell, and maybe fifteen of them take it.
You just increased your customer count by seventy five percent from people you were about to lose entirely.
Every one of those is additional cash, additional customers you can later upsell and rebill, and additional payback speed.
The downsell is how you stop leaving money on the table at the exact moment the table is fullest.
The fourth lever, and this is the one that builds durability, is continuity.
Continuity means recurring revenue.
Subscriptions, memberships, retainers, anything where the customer pays you again and again on a repeating schedule without you having to resell them every time.

This is the lever that transforms a business from something you have to feed constantly with new sales into something with a stable, predictable base of income that shows up whether or not you close a single new deal this month.
The power of continuity is twofold.
First, it stabilizes the whole business.
When you have a base of recurring revenue, you can plan.
You know that next month is going to start with a certain amount of money already flowing in.
That predictability is what lets you hire, invest, and take smart risks, because you are not starting from zero every thirty days.
Second, continuity is where the long term lifetime value of a customer really compounds.
A customer who pays you a hundred dollars a month and stays for two years is worth two thousand four hundred dollars.

That is the number that lets you afford to spend aggressively to acquire them in the first place, as long, and this is the key, as your money model gets enough of that value back quickly enough to keep the cash flowing.
Now, here is a subtle but important point Hormozi makes about how these levers work together.
There is a beautiful synergy between fast front end cash and long term continuity.
The attraction offer, the upsell, and the downsell are what get you your money back fast, in that critical thirty day window.
Continuity is what makes each customer enormously valuable over time.

So a great money model uses the fast levers to fund the acquisition, essentially getting the customer for free or better, and then the continuity runs in the background piling up profit month after month.
You get the speed and the durability.
You get paid back fast, and then you keep getting paid.
Let me pull all four levers into one worked example so you can see the whole machine turning.
Imagine you spend a hundred dollars in ads to acquire a customer.
Your front end attraction offer is a fifty dollar product, and it converts well because it is priced to be irresistible.
Right now you are fifty dollars in the hole, having spent a hundred and made back fifty.
But then, at the moment of purchase, you offer a two hundred dollar upsell, and enough people take it that on average you collect another seventy dollars per customer.
Now you have collected a hundred and twenty against your hundred dollar spend.
You are already ahead, in the first few minutes.
For the people who declined the fifty dollar front end, you offer a twenty dollar downsell, capturing revenue you would have lost.
And then, everyone who bought is rolled onto a fifty dollar a month membership.

So in month one, you have already made your acquisition cost back and then some, and starting in month two, every one of those customers is dropping fifty dollars into your account, month after month, for as long as they stay.
That is a money model.
That is the machine.
It funds its own growth on the front end and it compounds profit on the back end.
Now I want to spend some time on a distinction that Hormozi hammers, because it is the intellectual core of why this all works.
It is the difference between profit and cash flow, and more specifically, why the timing of cash is king for a growing company.
Profit is an accounting idea.
It is revenue minus expenses over some period.
It tells you whether, in theory, your business makes money.
But profit does not care about timing.
On an accounting statement, a dollar you will collect six months from now can look just as good as a dollar you have in your hand today.
Cash flow is different.
Cash flow is about the actual movement of money in and out of your bank account in real time.
And when you are growing, cash flow is the thing that keeps you alive or kills you, because you pay your bills, your ads, your team, and your suppliers with cash, not with theoretical future profit.
Here is the mental model that makes it click.
Imagine two businesses that are, on paper, exactly equally profitable.
Both of them, over the life of a customer, make a total profit of five hundred dollars per customer.
Identical profit.
But business A collects most of that five hundred dollars in the first thirty days.
Business B collects it slowly, twenty dollars a month over two years.
Which business can grow faster?
It is not close.
Business A can take the cash from each customer and immediately reinvest it into acquiring more customers, so it can double, and double again, funding the whole thing from customer cash.
Business B, with the same profit, is starved.
Every new customer it acquires deepens the cash hole, and to grow it has to keep finding outside money to bridge the gap.
Same profit.
Wildly different destinies.
And the only variable that changed was the timing of the cash.
This is why Hormozi says you can win, you can genuinely beat a competitor, even at the exact same total profit, simply by getting paid faster.
Speed of cash recovery is the hidden lever that almost nobody optimizes for, and it is the one that determines how fast you are allowed to grow.
When you restructure your offers so the cash comes back faster, you have not necessarily made more profit per customer.
You have made that profit available to you sooner, and available cash is what buys the next customer.
Speed is a weapon.
And this leads directly to what I think is the most strategically important idea in the entire book.
The person who can afford to spend the most to acquire a customer, and get that money back the fastest, wins the market.
Full stop.
Let me unpack why that is such a big deal.
Think about how customers get acquired in almost every market today.
You are competing for attention.
You are bidding, whether directly through ad auctions or indirectly through the quality and generosity of your offers, for the same eyeballs and the same leads that your competitors want.
Now, if you can afford to pay two hundred dollars to acquire a customer, and your competitor can only afford to pay fifty, who wins that auction?
You do, every single time.
You can outbid them for every click, every lead, every placement.

You can afford better ad spots, better sales teams, better everything, because you have a money model that lets you recover that two hundred dollars fast enough that spending it is not a risk, it is a reinvestment.
This is how the money model ties directly back to the "Leads" book.
In "Leads," Hormozi taught you how to get people to know about your offer through the four core methods and an army of lead getters.
But your ability to actually deploy all of that at scale is capped by how much you can afford to spend.
And how much you can afford to spend is determined entirely by your money model.
A great money model does not just make you more profitable.
It literally lets you afford customers your competitors cannot.
It expands the universe of leads you are able to pursue, because sources that are too expensive for a competitor with a weak money model are perfectly affordable for you.
You get to play in ad channels they cannot touch.
You get to be more generous with your offers than they can be.
You get to absorb a worse day, a slower month, a tougher market, because your cash comes back fast enough to keep you solvent.
Whoever has the best money model has the deepest pockets for acquisition, and in a war for attention, the deepest pockets win.
So the strategic goal becomes clear.
You want to stack these offers, the attraction, the upsell, the downsell, and the continuity, so that two numbers climb at the same time.
The first number is your average revenue per customer, how much a typical customer is worth to you across that whole sequence.
The second number is the speed of payback, how fast that money lands in your account.
Every offer you add to the sequence should be evaluated on both.
Does it raise the average value of a customer, and does it get cash into my hands faster?

When you stack offers well, both numbers rise together, and as they rise, your capacity to spend on acquisition rises with them, and you pull further and further ahead of anyone who has not done this work.
Now let me bring in guarantees and risk reversal, because they play a specific and important role inside a money model, and it is not the role you might expect.
Most people think of a guarantee as a defensive thing, a way to reassure nervous customers.
And it is that.
But in the context of a money model, a guarantee is also an offensive weapon for conversion and for speed.
Here is the logic.
The biggest thing stopping a stranger from buying, especially on your front end offer, is risk.
They do not know you.
They do not know if it will work.
So every dollar of perceived risk you can strip away increases the percentage of people who say yes.
And a strong guarantee, an unconditional refund, a win your money back promise, a we will keep working until you get the result, transfers that risk from the customer's shoulders onto yours.
When you take on the risk, more people buy, and they buy faster, and they buy at higher prices, because the downside they were afraid of is gone.
That directly feeds your money model.
More conversions and higher prices on the front end mean more cash in the first thirty days.
Now, the fear every owner has is that a big guarantee means everyone will ask for a refund and you will go broke.

But Hormozi's experience, and the experience of most people who actually test this, is that a well constructed guarantee attached to a genuinely good offer increases total profit, because the lift in the number of people who buy vastly outweighs the small number who ask for their money back.
The guarantee is not charity.
It is a lever that raises your front end conversion, and front end conversion is what feeds the whole machine.
The key is to pair aggressive risk reversal with an offer and a product that actually deliver, so that you earn the extra sales without drowning in refunds.
I want to make sure we do not lose the forest for the trees here, so let me zoom out to the ethos of the whole thing, because Hormozi is very deliberate that this is not a book of gimmicks.
It would be easy to read about upsells and downsells and win your money back offers and think, okay, these are clever little tricks to squeeze more money out of people.
That is not the spirit of the book, and honestly, that mindset will fail you, because tricks do not survive contact with real customers over time.
The entire point of a money model is to build a real, durable, cash generating machine.

A business that creates so much genuine value that customers happily pay you fast and pay you again, and that recovers its cash quickly enough that it can grow relentlessly without ever running out of fuel.
Every lever we talked about only works if the underlying value is real.
The upsell works because it genuinely helps the customer get a better result.
The continuity works because the customer keeps getting value month after month, which is why they stay.
The guarantee works because the product actually delivers, so the refunds stay low.
Strip out the value and the whole thing collapses.
So the money model is not a way to extract more from people.
It is a way to structure a fundamentally generous, valuable business so that its economics let it win.
You make more money precisely because you have engineered more ways to deliver more value at the moments the customer most wants it.
And this is where the whole series comes together into one picture.
In "Offers," you built a Grand Slam Offer, something so valuable people feel dumb saying no.
In "Leads," you opened the floodgates, you learned how to get an unlimited stream of strangers to know that offer exists.
And now, in "Money Models," you have learned how to structure the money so that turning those leads into customers pays for itself fast enough to fund infinite growth.
Put those three together and you have the full engine.
A world class offer, a flood of leads pointed at it, and a money model that recovers your cash faster than anyone else in your market can.
That is the complete Acquisition dot com machine.

Each book is one third of a single system, and "Money Models" is the third that most people are missing, which is exactly why most people stay stuck no matter how good their product is or how much traffic they get.
Let me also address a question that is probably forming in your mind right now, which is, does this only work for online businesses selling courses and coaching?
And the answer is no.
The principles are universal, because the underlying problem is universal.
Every business, whether it is a gym, a landscaping company, a software company, a dental practice, or an ecommerce brand, spends money to get customers and collects money from them over time.
Every one of them faces the cash timing gap.
So every one of them can build a money model.

The gym can have a low friction front end trial, an upsell to personal training at the point of sign up, a downsell to a smaller class package for the people who balk at the full membership, and continuity through the monthly membership itself.

The landscaper can have an attraction offer of a cheap first service, an upsell to a seasonal package right when the customer is delighted, a downsell payment plan for the big project, and continuity through a recurring maintenance contract.
Same four levers, completely different industry.
The shapes change, the logic does not.
Now, before we get to your action list, let me consolidate the mental model one more time, because repetition is how this becomes instinct.
The single question that governs everything is, how fast do I get my acquisition cost back?
You want that answer to be measured in days, not months.
You get there by stacking offers in a deliberate sequence.
You use an attraction offer, often priced just to cover your ad spend, to convert strangers into buyers with as little friction as possible.
You use an upsell at the moment of purchase, when buying momentum is highest, to stack instant high margin revenue on top.
You use a downsell to rescue revenue from the people who said no, capturing customers you already paid to reach.
And you use continuity to build a predictable, compounding base of recurring income that makes each customer enormously valuable over their lifetime.
You wrap the front end in a strong guarantee to boost conversion.
And you keep your eye fixed not on profit alone, but on cash timing, because whoever recovers cash the fastest can spend the most to acquire customers, and whoever can spend the most, wins.
So let me leave you with something concrete, a set of moves you can make this week on your own money model.
Not someday.
This week.
First, sit down and calculate your actual customer acquisition cost and your actual thirty day cash.
Add up everything you spend to get one customer.
Then look at exactly how much money a typical customer gives you in their first thirty days.
Put those two numbers next to each other.
If your thirty day cash is less than your acquisition cost, you have found your bottleneck, and everything that follows is about closing that gap.
Most owners have never done this simple calculation, and it is often a wake up call.
Do it first, because you cannot fix what you have not measured.
Second, design or sharpen one upsell that you can present at the exact moment a customer buys.
Ask yourself, what is the one thing that would make what they just bought work even better, or faster, or with less effort on their part?
Build that offer and put it right at the point of sale, immediately after the yes, while the momentum is hot.
This is usually the single fastest lever to add, and it drops straight into your thirty day cash.
Third, create one downsell for the people who say no to your main offer.
It can be a payment plan, a smaller version, or a lighter first step.
Pick whichever is easiest to launch, and give your prospects a second yes to say instead of walking away.
You already paid to get them in front of you.
Give them a way to become a customer.
Fourth, look hard at whether you have any continuity in your business, any recurring revenue at all.
If you do not, brainstorm one way to add a subscription, a membership, a maintenance plan, a retainer, or a recurring service.
Recurring revenue is the foundation of stability, and even a small continuity offer changes the entire risk profile of your company.
If you already have continuity, spend this week on one idea to reduce the number of people who cancel, because every month you keep a customer is another month of nearly free profit.
Fifth, examine your front end offer and ask whether it is priced and structured to lower the barrier to that first yes as much as possible.
Could you add a stronger guarantee?
Could you restructure it into a liquidating offer that covers your ad spend so your acquisition becomes effectively free at the point of sale?
Make one change this week that makes it easier and less risky for a stranger to buy from you the first time.
And sixth, once you have your levers in place, do the most important thing of all.
Reinvest the cash.
The entire point of getting your money back fast is to turn around and use it to acquire more customers.
So as your thirty day cash climbs above your acquisition cost, take that surplus and put it right back into getting more leads and more customers.
That is the flywheel.
Customers fund acquisition, acquisition brings more customers, more customers fund more acquisition, and around it spins, faster and faster, funded entirely by itself.
Here is the truth to carry with you.
Your competitors are almost certainly not thinking about any of this.
They are worried about their product, their logo, their website, their profit margin on paper.
They are not thinking about the sequence of offers or the speed of cash recovery.
Which means this is your edge.

A business with an ordinary offer and an extraordinary money model will crush a business with an extraordinary offer and no money model, because the one with the money model can afford to acquire every customer in the market and the other one runs out of cash trying.
So do the math on your thirty day cash this week.
Add one upsell, one downsell, and, if you can, one continuity offer.
Strengthen your front end with a guarantee.
And then reinvest every dollar of surplus into growth.
Do that, and you stop being a business that grows until it runs out of cash.
You become a business that grows because it generates cash.
And that, more than any single product or clever ad, is what lets you build something that lasts, and lets you outspend, outlast, and outgrow everyone in your market.
That is "100 Million Dollar Money Models" by Alex Hormozi.
Take one of those six actions today, before the momentum of this conversation fades.
Because knowing the money model is not what changes your business.
Building it is.
Now go build it.
